news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in AI-assisted scientific research, discoveries advancing quantum science, innovative redevelopment plans for Europe's oldest science park, and revised understanding of fundamental particle physics.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la recherche scientifique assistée par l'IA, des découvertes qui font progresser la science quantique, des projets de réaménagement innovants pour le plus ancien parc scientifique d'Europe, ainsi qu'une nouvelle compréhension de la physique des particules fondamentales.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en la investigación científica asistida por IA, los descubrimientos que impulsan la ciencia cuántica, los innovadores planes de remodelación del parque científico más antiguo de Europa y una nueva visión de la física de partículas fundamentales.

articles.title: An AI system to help scientists write expert-level empirical software
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10658-6An AI system to help scientists write expert-level empirical software
articles.content: The cycle of scientific discovery is frequently bottlenecked by the slow, manual creation of software to support computational experiments1. To address this, we present Empirical Research Assistance (ERA), an AI system that creates expert-level scientific software whose goal is to maximize a quality metric. The system uses a Large Language Model (LLM) and Tree Search (TS)2 to systematically improve the quality metric and intelligently navigate the large space of possible solutions. ERA achieves expert-level results when it explores and integrates complex research ideas from external sources. The effectiveness of tree search is demonstrated across a diverse range of tasks. In bioinformatics, ERA discovered 40 novel methods for single-cell data analysis that outperformed the top human-developed methods on a public leaderboard. In epidemiology, ERA generated 14 models that outperformed the CDC ensemble and all other individual models for forecasting COVID-19 hospitalizations. ERA also produced expert-level software for geospatial analysis, neural activity prediction in zebrafish, and numerical solution of integrals, and a novel rule-based construction for time series forecasting. By devising and implementing novel solutions to diverse tasks, ERA represents a significant step towards accelerating scientific progress.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant AI breakthrough that accelerates scientific discovery by automating expert-level software creation across multiple scientific domains, leading to improved methods and models that outperform existing human-developed solutions. This advancement has broad, meaningful impact on scientific research and public health forecasting, demonstrating substantial real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: New AI system helps scientists create expert-level research software faster
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often spend a lot of time writing computer programs to analyze data and run experiments. This process can be slow and hold back new discoveries.

**What happened** – Researchers developed an AI system called Empirical Research Assistance (ERA) that can write advanced scientific software on its own. ERA uses a type of AI called a Large Language Model combined with a method called Tree Search to find the best solutions. It was tested in different fields like biology, disease forecasting, and data analysis.

**Impact** – ERA created new software tools that worked better than those made by human experts. For example, it found 40 new ways to study single cells that topped public leaderboards and made 14 models that predicted COVID-19 hospital hospitalizations more accurately than the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). This shows ERA can speed up research and help improve important health predictions.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to improve ERA and use it in more areas of research. This could help discover new methods faster and support better decision-making in science and health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – An AI named ERA can now write expert scientific software faster and better than humans, helping speed up important research and health forecasting.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau système d'IA aide les scientifiques à créer plus rapidement des logiciels de recherche experts
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques passent souvent beaucoup de temps à écrire des programmes informatiques pour analyser des données et réaliser des expériences. Ce processus peut être lent et ralentir les découvertes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont développé un système d'IA appelé Empirical Research Assistance (ERA) capable d'écrire seul des logiciels scientifiques avancés. ERA utilise un type d'IA nommé grand modèle de langage combiné à une méthode appelée recherche arborescente pour trouver les meilleures solutions. Il a été testé dans différents domaines comme la biologie, la prévision des maladies et l'analyse de données.

**Impact** – ERA a créé de nouveaux outils logiciels qui fonctionnent mieux que ceux conçus par des experts humains. Par exemple, il a trouvé 40 nouvelles méthodes pour étudier les cellules individuelles qui ont surpassé les meilleurs résultats publics et a produit 14 modèles prédisant les hospitalisations COVID-19 plus précisément que le CDC. Cela montre qu'ERA peut accélérer la recherche et améliorer les prévisions de santé importantes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à améliorer ERA et l'utiliser dans plus de domaines de recherche. Cela pourrait permettre de découvrir plus vite de nouvelles méthodes et d'aider à de meilleures décisions en science et santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une IA nommée ERA peut désormais écrire des logiciels scientifiques experts plus vite et mieux que les humains, accélérant la recherche et les prévisions en santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo sistema de IA ayuda a científicos a crear software experto más rápido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos a menudo dedican mucho tiempo a escribir programas de computadora para analizar datos y realizar experimentos. Este proceso puede ser lento y retrasar nuevos descubrimientos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores desarrollaron un sistema de IA llamado Empirical Research Assistance (ERA) que puede escribir software científico avanzado por sí solo. ERA usa un tipo de IA llamado Modelo de Lenguaje Grande junto con un método llamado Búsqueda en Árbol para encontrar las mejores soluciones. Fue probado en campos como biología, predicción de enfermedades y análisis de datos.

**Impacto** – ERA creó nuevas herramientas de software que funcionaron mejor que las hechas por expertos humanos. Por ejemplo, encontró 40 nuevas formas de estudiar células individuales que superaron los mejores resultados públicos y generó 14 modelos que predijeron hospitalizaciones por COVID-19 con mayor precisión que el Centro para el Control y la Prevención de Enfermedades (CDC). Esto muestra que ERA puede acelerar la investigación y mejorar predicciones importantes de salud.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán mejorando ERA y usándolo en más áreas de investigación. Esto podría ayudar a descubrir métodos nuevos más rápido y apoyar mejores decisiones en ciencia y salud.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una IA llamada ERA ahora puede escribir software científico experto más rápido y mejor que los humanos, ayudando a acelerar la investigación y la predicción en salud.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an intricate, glowing tree made of interconnected circuit-like branches and scientific symbols, with radiant light nodes representing AI-driven discovery, surrounded by softly colored abstract representations of data streams and diverse scientific fields like bioinformatics and epidemiology, all blending harmoniously in natural, muted tones.

articles.title: Odd “butterfly” molecule could lead to new parts of the quantum realm
articles.summary: An exotic new molecule is shaped like a butterfly, complete with "wings" made from electrons. The discovery could provide a gateway to completely new parts of the quantum realm
articles.content:
An exotic new molecule is shaped like a butterfly, complete with "wings" made from electrons. The discovery could provide a gateway to completely new parts of the quantum realm

A laser system used to create butterfly molecules Prof. Herwig Ott

A large, cold molecule that resembles a butterfly, with “wings” made from electrons, has been made for the first time, completing the search for a “zoo” of similar molecules. The result could provide a gateway to completely new parts of the quantum realm.

Herwig Ott at RPTU University Kaiserslautern-Landau in Germany and his colleagues made the molecule by cooling rubidium atoms to a few millionths of a degree above absolute zero by using lasers and electromagnetic forces. The researchers then used lasers again to make some atoms very large by pushing their outermost electron very far from their nuclei. The quantum properties of atoms that have been cooled and enlarged in this way can be precisely manipulated with lasers, which the team leveraged to move a giant atom’s electron towards a normal-sized rubidium atom, binding them together to create a new type of molecule with extreme properties.

Each molecule was about 25 nanometres in size – bigger than the diameter of a DNA strand that contains billions of atoms – and thousands of times more responsive to electric fields than most molecules. The shape of the new molecule was determined by its electrons, with the outermost being spread out in space in a shape that resembles the wings of a butterfly.

Ott says that tuning the lasers just right to get molecules with this exact configuration was tricky – it took weeks of tweaking before the team successfully created them in the lab. Ott compares the process to searching for an object on a road by inspecting 1 millimetre at a time while standing a kilometre away.

Team member Matthew Eiles at Purdue University in Indiana says the work is a culmination of several past mathematical and experimental studies that helped narrow down this search. Based on mathematical models, researchers have spent 20 years searching for a “zoo” of giant ultracold molecules ought to exist, and the new butterfly is the last one to be discovered, he says.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

At the same time, this experiment opens a path towards creating more exotic and elusive ultracold molecules, including those that could be very heavy in addition to being very large and charged, says Michał Tomza at the University of Warsaw in Poland. Weibin Li at the University of Nottingham in the UK says the butterfly molecule was difficult to create but could now be used as precursor for making something even more difficult – ultracold atoms with negative charge, or anions. If made ultracold, anions could be used in tests of fundamental laws of particle physics or antimatter studies, but standard cooling methods have failed to chill them so far.

Eiles and Ott have already mathematically investigated exactly how to use the butterfly molecule to create ultracold anions and hope to see the first signs of them within just a few years. “The theory is already written,” says Eiles.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in quantum physics with the creation of a novel 'butterfly' molecule. This discovery opens new pathways in quantum research, potentially enabling advances in fundamental physics and antimatter studies. The impact is broad for the scientific community and future technology development, with detailed context and significance provided.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create a new 'butterfly' molecule that opens doors to quantum discoveries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been searching for special, very cold and very large molecules in the quantum world for about 20 years. These molecules behave in unusual ways because of the strange rules of quantum physics.

**What happened** – Researchers in Germany cooled rubidium atoms to nearly absolute zero using lasers and magnets. They then made some atoms very large by pushing one of their electrons far away from the center. By carefully using lasers, they joined a giant atom and a normal atom to form a new molecule shaped like a butterfly, with its “wings” made by spread-out electrons.

**Impact** – This new molecule is much bigger than typical molecules and reacts strongly to electric fields. It is the last expected member of a group of giant molecules scientists predicted but had not found. This discovery gives scientists a new tool to explore unusual quantum effects and could help create even stranger molecules, like ultracold negatively charged atoms called anions, which are hard to make but important for studying fundamental physics.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to use the butterfly molecule to create ultracold anions in the next few years. These anions could help test basic laws of physics and antimatter, opening new areas of science that were difficult to study before.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have made a unique butterfly-shaped molecule that could lead to new quantum experiments and discoveries about the nature of matter and antimatter.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une nouvelle molécule en forme de papillon qui ouvre la voie à des découvertes quantiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis environ 20 ans, les scientifiques cherchent des molécules très froides et très grandes dans le monde quantique. Ces molécules ont des comportements étranges à cause des règles particulières de la physique quantique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs en Allemagne ont refroidi des atomes de rubidium presque jusqu’au zéro absolu grâce à des lasers et des aimants. Ils ont ensuite agrandi certains atomes en éloignant fortement un de leurs électrons. En utilisant les lasers avec précision, ils ont lié un atome géant et un atome normal pour créer une nouvelle molécule en forme de papillon, avec des « ailes » formées par des électrons étalés.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle molécule est beaucoup plus grande que les molécules habituelles et réagit fortement aux champs électriques. C’est le dernier membre attendu d’un groupe de molécules géantes prédites mais jamais découvertes. Cette découverte donne aux scientifiques un nouvel outil pour explorer des phénomènes quantiques inhabituels et pourrait permettre de créer des molécules encore plus étranges, comme des atomes ultrafroids chargés négativement appelés anions, difficiles à fabriquer mais importants pour étudier la physique fondamentale.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit d’utiliser la molécule papillon pour créer des anions ultrafroids dans les prochaines années. Ces anions pourraient aider à tester les lois de base de la physique et l’antimatière, ouvrant de nouvelles voies scientifiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont fabriqué une molécule unique en forme de papillon qui pourrait mener à de nouvelles expériences quantiques et découvertes sur la matière et l’antimatière.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean una nueva molécula en forma de mariposa que abre puertas a descubrimientos cuánticos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante unos 20 años, los científicos han buscado moléculas muy frías y muy grandes en el mundo cuántico. Estas moléculas se comportan de forma extraña debido a las reglas especiales de la física cuántica.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Alemania enfriaron átomos de rubidio a casi el cero absoluto usando láseres y campos magnéticos. Luego hicieron que algunos átomos fueran muy grandes al alejar mucho uno de sus electrones. Con láseres, unieron un átomo gigante y uno normal para crear una nueva molécula con forma de mariposa, con “alas” hechas por electrones extendidos.

**Impacto** – Esta molécula nueva es mucho más grande que las comunes y responde mucho a los campos eléctricos. Es el último miembro esperado de un grupo de moléculas gigantes que los científicos predijeron pero no habían encontrado. Este descubrimiento da a los científicos una herramienta nueva para explorar efectos cuánticos raros y podría ayudar a crear moléculas aún más extrañas, como átomos ultrafríos con carga negativa llamados aniones, que son difíciles de hacer pero importantes para estudiar la física fundamental.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea usar la molécula mariposa para crear aniones ultrafríos en los próximos años. Estos aniones podrían ayudar a probar las leyes básicas de la física y el estudio de la antimateria, abriendo nuevas áreas científicas.

**Frase resumen** – Científicos han creado una molécula única en forma de mariposa que podría llevar a nuevos experimentos cuánticos y descubrimientos sobre la materia y la antimateria.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, glowing abstract molecule shaped like a butterfly with delicate, translucent electron "wings" made of swirling light and energy, hovering above a softly lit laboratory table with subtle laser beams gently illuminating the scene, all rendered in natural, soft colors with an ethereal, quantum-inspired atmosphere.

articles.title: Europe's oldest science park could be redeveloped
articles.summary: Cambridge Science Park submits plans which could create 20,000 jobs and new public spaces.
articles.content: In the proposals, the amount of built space would increase from 2.8 million to 8 million square feet. The college said it was determined to ensure that any growth "provides benefits and opportunities for everyone in Cambridge, Peterborough and surrounding towns and villages".
articles.reasoning: The redevelopment of Europe's oldest science park with plans to create 20,000 jobs and new public spaces represents a significant positive real-world impact with broad benefits for the local community and economy. The article provides clear context about the scale of expansion and the inclusive growth intentions, making it a meaningful and inspiring development in technology and innovation infrastructure.
articles.personality_title: Cambridge Science Park plans major expansion to create 20,000 jobs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cambridge Science Park is Europe's oldest science park, known for supporting technology and innovation companies. It has been an important place for research and business for many years.

**What happened** – The park's owners have submitted plans to expand the site from 2.8 million to 8 million square feet of built space. This redevelopment aims to create 20,000 new jobs and add public spaces for the community. The plans also focus on benefiting Cambridge, nearby Peterborough, and surrounding towns and villages.

**Impact** – This expansion is significant because it could greatly boost the local economy by providing thousands of new jobs. It also plans to improve public areas, making the space more welcoming for residents and workers. The project balances growth with community needs, which is less common in large developments.

**What's next step** – The plans will now be reviewed by local authorities and the public can give feedback. If approved, construction will begin, and over time the park will grow to include new buildings and spaces.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cambridge Science Park’s expansion could bring thousands of jobs and new community spaces while supporting inclusive growth in the region.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le parc scientifique de Cambridge prévoit une grande expansion créant 20 000 emplois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le parc scientifique de Cambridge est le plus ancien parc scientifique d'Europe, connu pour soutenir les entreprises de technologie et d'innovation. Il joue un rôle important dans la recherche et les affaires depuis de nombreuses années.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les propriétaires du parc ont soumis des plans pour agrandir le site, passant de 2,8 millions à 8 millions de pieds carrés d'espace construit. Cette rénovation vise à créer 20 000 nouveaux emplois et à ajouter des espaces publics pour la communauté. Les projets mettent aussi l'accent sur les bénéfices pour Cambridge, Peterborough et les villes et villages environnants.

**Impact** – Cette expansion est importante car elle pourrait renforcer l'économie locale en offrant des milliers de nouveaux emplois. Elle prévoit aussi d'améliorer les espaces publics pour les habitants et les travailleurs. Le projet cherche un équilibre entre croissance et besoins de la communauté, ce qui est rare dans les grands développements.

**Prochaine étape** – Les plans vont maintenant être examinés par les autorités locales et le public pourra donner son avis. Si le projet est approuvé, la construction commencera et le parc s'agrandira avec de nouveaux bâtiments et espaces.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'expansion du parc scientifique de Cambridge pourrait créer des milliers d'emplois et de nouveaux espaces communautaires tout en soutenant une croissance inclusive dans la région.
articles.personality_title_es: El parque científico de Cambridge planea gran expansión para crear 20,000 empleos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El parque científico de Cambridge es el parque científico más antiguo de Europa, conocido por apoyar a empresas de tecnología e innovación. Ha sido un lugar importante para la investigación y los negocios durante muchos años.

**Qué pasó** – Los dueños del parque han presentado planes para ampliar el sitio de 2.8 millones a 8 millones de pies cuadrados de espacio construido. Esta renovación busca crear 20,000 nuevos empleos y añadir espacios públicos para la comunidad. Los planes también se enfocan en beneficiar a Cambridge, Peterborough y a las ciudades y pueblos cercanos.

**Impacto** – Esta expansión es importante porque podría impulsar mucho la economía local al ofrecer miles de nuevos empleos. También planea mejorar los espacios públicos, haciendo el lugar más acogedor para residentes y trabajadores. El proyecto busca equilibrar el crecimiento con las necesidades de la comunidad, algo poco común en desarrollos grandes.

**Próximo paso** – Los planes serán revisados por las autoridades locales y el público podrá dar su opinión. Si se aprueban, comenzará la construcción y con el tiempo el parque crecerá con nuevos edificios y espacios.

**Resumen en una frase** – La expansión del parque científico de Cambridge podría generar miles de empleos y nuevos espacios comunitarios mientras apoya un crecimiento inclusivo en la región.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an expansive, evolving science park represented by interconnected, glowing geometric structures gradually growing in size and complexity across a gently rolling landscape, with stylized silhouettes of Cambridge and Peterborough landmarks in the distance under a soft, natural color palette.

articles.title: Scientists were wrong about this “rule-breaking” particle
articles.summary: Scientists spent decades chasing signs of a mysterious new force hidden inside the muon, one of nature’s strangest particles. But after years of supercomputer calculations, researchers discovered the apparent anomaly was likely a calculation error — and the Standard Model still reigns supreme.
articles.content:
For decades, a puzzling discrepancy involving a tiny subatomic particle called the muon fueled speculation that physicists might be on the verge of discovering an entirely new force of nature. Now, an international research team led by a Penn State physicist says the mystery appears to have been solved, and the answer supports existing physics rather than overturning it.

The researchers published their findings in the journal Nature, describing one of the most precise particle physics calculations ever completed. Their work shows that the long debated mismatch between theory and experiment was likely caused by limitations in earlier calculations rather than evidence of unknown physics.

Decades of Hopes for "New Physics"

The mystery centered on the muon, a short lived particle that resembles an electron but is about 200 times heavier. For more than 60 years, measurements of the muon's magnetic behavior appeared to disagree with predictions made by the Standard Model, the framework scientists use to describe the universe's fundamental particles and forces.

That discrepancy excited physicists because it hinted at the possibility of undiscovered particles or even a new "fifth force" beyond the four known fundamental forces.

"There were many calculations in the last 60 years or so, and as they got more and more precise they all pointed toward a discrepancy and a new interaction that would upend known laws of physics," said Zoltan Fodor, distinguished professor of physics at Penn State and lead author of the study. "We applied a new method to calculate this discrepancy quantity, and we showed that it's not there. This new interaction we hoped for simply is not there. The old interactions can explain the value completely."

The team spent more than a decade refining the calculation. Their final result brought theoretical predictions and experimental measurements into agreement within less than half a standard deviation. According to Fodor, the new work confirms the Standard Model to 11 decimal places and significantly narrows the chances that unknown physics is hiding in this particular measurement.

"People ask me how it feels to make this discovery and, to be honest, I feel somewhat sad," Fodor said. "When we started to calculate this quantity, we thought we were going to have a good and trustworthy calculation for a new fifth force. Instead, we found there is no fifth force. We did find a very precise proof of not just the Standard Model, but also of quantum field theory, which is the foundation on which the Standard Model was built."

The Muon's Strange Magnetic Behavior

The research focused on a property known as the muon's magnetic moment, which describes how strongly the particle acts like a tiny magnet. Quantum theory predicts that the value should equal exactly two, representing the relationship between the particle's wobble and the magnetic field surrounding it.

In real experiments, however, the value shifts slightly because other particles briefly appear and disappear in empty space, subtly affecting the muon's behavior. This tiny deviation is known as the "anomalous magnetic moment," or g−2.

Because muons are much heavier than electrons, they are especially sensitive to these fleeting quantum effects. That sensitivity has made muon g−2 one of the most closely studied measurements in modern physics.

Experiments performed at CERN in the 1960s and 1970s, later at Brookhaven National Laboratory, and more recently at Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory all measured the muon's magnetic moment with remarkable precision. Those experiments recently earned the Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics, one of the world's most prestigious science awards.

For years, the experimental measurements continued to appear inconsistent with Standard Model predictions, strengthening hopes that something entirely new might be influencing the muon.

Why the Strong Force Made the Problem So Difficult

The challenge in calculating the muon's behavior came largely from the strong force, the most powerful of the four known fundamental forces. The strong force binds quarks together inside protons, neutrons, and other particles.

Unlike gravity or electromagnetism, the strong force becomes stronger as particles move farther apart, similar to a rubber band stretching tighter the more it is pulled. Attempting to separate particles connected by the strong force requires so much energy that entirely new particles can form during the process. Those additional particles further complicate calculations.

Because of this extreme complexity, accurately predicting the muon's behavior within the Standard Model has remained one of the most difficult problems in particle physics.

Supercomputers and Lattice Quantum Chromodynamics

To tackle the problem, the researchers relied on lattice quantum chromodynamics, a computational technique that simulates the strong force using enormous supercomputers. The method divides space and time into an extremely fine grid, or lattice, allowing scientists to numerically solve the equations governing particle interactions.

"The old methodology involved collecting thousands of experimental results and reinterpreting them to get the single number, the magnetic moment of the muon," Fodor said. "Our approach was completely different. We divided space time into very small cells, a lattice, then we solved the equations of the Standard Model on that. There was an awful lot of theory, mathematics, programming, computational knowledge and computer architecture behind this calculation."

Over the past decade, lattice calculations have become increasingly powerful, but the precision needed for the muon g−2 calculation remained exceptionally difficult to achieve. The team therefore combined several approaches.

They used lattice calculations for short and medium distances between the cells while incorporating highly reliable experimental measurements for larger distances where existing data already agreed strongly. This hybrid strategy reduced uncertainty more effectively than relying on either method alone.

The researchers also simulated the equations using finer lattices than previous studies, further improving precision and reducing possible errors.

The final calculation represents the most accurate determination yet of the muon's magnetic moment. When incorporated into the full Standard Model prediction, the longstanding disagreement with experiments essentially disappears.

"The prediction combines electromagnetic, weak and strong forces, that each require vastly different theoretical tools, into a single calculation that's accurate to parts per billion," Fodor said. "It shows that we really do understand how nature works at an incredibly deep level."

What the Result Means for Physics

The findings do not completely rule out the possibility of undiscovered physics, according to the researchers. However, one of the strongest potential clues pointing beyond the Standard Model has now become far less convincing.

Future experiments may still uncover evidence of new particles or forces elsewhere, but for now, the Standard Model continues to withstand intense scrutiny.

"We didn't get the fifth force, but we did get a very nice and probably the best proof of quantum theory, which is the underlying theory of all our understanding of the most fundamental questions of nature," Fodor said.

The Penn State portion of the research was supported by the U.S. Department of Energy and the European Research Council.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement: resolving a decades-long physics anomaly by using advanced computational methods to confirm the Standard Model with unprecedented precision. This breakthrough enhances our fundamental understanding of particle physics, demonstrating progress in scientific knowledge with broad implications for the field and society's grasp of the universe's laws.
articles.personality_title: New calculations confirm the muon's behavior fits known physics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For over 60 years, scientists noticed a strange difference between what they expected and what they measured about a tiny particle called the muon. This difference made them wonder if there was a new force in nature beyond the four we already know.

**What happened** – A team led by a physicist at Penn State used powerful supercomputers and a method called lattice quantum chromodynamics to recalculate the muon's magnetic behavior. Their work, published in Nature, showed that earlier results were likely wrong because of calculation limits. Their new, very precise results matched what the Standard Model of physics predicts.

**Impact** – This discovery means the muon does not reveal a new force after all. Instead, it confirms that our current understanding of particles and forces is very accurate, down to 11 decimal places. The finding also shows how advanced computer methods can solve tough problems in physics.

**What's next step** – Scientists will keep searching for new physics in other experiments, but this result narrows where surprises might be found. The techniques used here will help improve calculations in other areas of particle physics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Careful new calculations show the muon behaves exactly as current physics predicts, solving a decades-old mystery about a possible new force.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux calculs confirment que le comportement du muon suit la physique connue
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus de 60 ans, les scientifiques ont remarqué une différence étrange entre ce qu’ils attendaient et ce qu’ils mesuraient à propos d’une petite particule appelée le muon. Cette différence les a fait se demander s’il existait une nouvelle force dans la nature, au-delà des quatre connues.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par un physicien de Penn State a utilisé de puissants supercalculateurs et une méthode appelée chromodynamique quantique sur réseau pour recalculer le comportement magnétique du muon. Leur travail, publié dans Nature, a montré que les résultats antérieurs étaient probablement erronés à cause de limites dans les calculs. Leurs nouveaux résultats très précis correspondent aux prévisions du Modèle Standard de la physique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte signifie que le muon ne révèle pas une nouvelle force. Au lieu de cela, elle confirme que notre compréhension actuelle des particules et des forces est très précise, jusqu’à 11 décimales. Ce résultat montre aussi comment des méthodes informatiques avancées peuvent résoudre des problèmes difficiles en physique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront à chercher une nouvelle physique dans d’autres expériences, mais ce résultat réduit les endroits où des surprises pourraient être trouvées. Les techniques utilisées ici aideront à améliorer les calculs dans d’autres domaines de la physique des particules.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouveaux calculs précis montrent que le muon se comporte exactement comme la physique actuelle le prédit, résolvant un mystère vieux de plusieurs décennies sur une possible nouvelle force.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos cálculos confirman que el comportamiento del muón sigue la física conocida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 60 años, los científicos notaron una extraña diferencia entre lo que esperaban y lo que medían sobre una pequeña partícula llamada muón. Esta diferencia hizo que se preguntaran si existía una nueva fuerza en la naturaleza, más allá de las cuatro que ya conocemos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo dirigido por un físico de Penn State utilizó potentes supercomputadoras y un método llamado cromodinámica cuántica en red para recalcular el comportamiento magnético del muón. Su trabajo, publicado en Nature, mostró que los resultados anteriores probablemente estaban equivocados debido a límites en los cálculos. Sus nuevos resultados, muy precisos, coincidieron con lo que predice el Modelo Estándar de la física.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento significa que el muón no revela una nueva fuerza. En cambio, confirma que nuestra comprensión actual de las partículas y fuerzas es muy precisa, hasta 11 decimales. El hallazgo también muestra cómo los métodos avanzados por computadora pueden resolver problemas difíciles en física.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán buscando nueva física en otros experimentos, pero este resultado reduce los lugares donde podrían encontrarse sorpresas. Las técnicas usadas aquí ayudarán a mejorar cálculos en otras áreas de la física de partículas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cálculos cuidadosos muestran que el muón se comporta exactamente como predice la física actual, resolviendo un misterio de décadas sobre una posible nueva fuerza.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately woven lattice grid floating in a soft cosmic space, with a radiant, stylized muon particle at its center emitting gentle magnetic waves, surrounded by subtle abstract representations of quantum fields and forces harmoniously interacting.
